candidates.title: 'I photographed Bob Dylan as fan shouted Judas at Manchester gig'
candidates.summary: Photographer and fan Alan Corbett took shots of Dylan from the balcony at Manchester Free Trade Hall
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review. | Validator: editorial_contract rejected item (story_type=news, anchor=none, tier=filler).
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: Photographer and fan Alan Corbett took shots of Dylan from the balcony at Manchester Free Trade Hall
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Paul Burnell North West Alan Corbett The notorious heckle in Manchester is part of rock folklore Music fan Alan Corbett had no idea he would be snapping a piece of pop history when he took a camera to a Bob Dylan gig at Manchester Free Trade Corbett, then aged 19, had seen Dylan perform an acoustic set at the venue a year earlier on 7 May where he had seen his hero from the first two rows supported by the legendary Joan Baez The following year though, it was a different story in more ways than one For starters, Corbett was up on the balcony, but more memorably, in the second half of his set, Dylan had strapped a 1965 black Fender Telecaster electric guitar to himself "He was so popular, the only places we could get were in the balcony," said Corbett The fledgling photographer was still smarting from music newspaper Melody Maker rejecting his shots of jazz luminary Ornette Coleman when he took his camera into the gig

candidates.lead: With mayors able to introduce an extra tax, two candidates say they would not bring it in if elected
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Kaleigh Watterson Cheshire political reporter BBC, Labour Ben Fletcher and Dan Price have both been selected for the election next year The Conservative and Labour candidates standing to become mayor of Cheshire and Warrington have said they Elected mayors across England have the ability to raise a mayoral precept - a sum on top of council tax to raise additional funds for services - although most of them currently do not Conservative Ben Fletcher said "taxing people more was not the answer" and in response, Labour's Dan Price said he also had "no plans to introduce tax" The other major parties have not yet named their candidates The Cheshire and Warrington Combined Authority came into force earlier this year and the mayoral election will take place next year Businessman Fletcher said he would use his commercial experience to "lead an efficient and effective combined authority that creates plans that win private investment"

candidates.lead: Kate White says she is "extremely concerned about the inability to get resources" to the Democratic Republic of Congo
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Jen Sugden and Rumeana Jahangir , North West BBC Kate White, from Médecins Sans Frontières, flew from Manchester Airport as part of an international relief effort The Ebola outbreak is posing massive challenges for medical aid organisations, Kate White, who is a programme manager for the charity Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF), and is flying out to the Democratic Republic of Congo, says she is "extremely concerned about the inability to get resources" to the country Three Red Cross volunteers who died earlier this month were among the first known victims of DR Congo's Ebola outbreak, and likely caught it while managing dead bodies The outbreak has resulted in more than 200 suspected deaths and more than 850 suspected cases White, who flew from Manchester Airport on Sunday as part of an international relief effort, said: "It really reinforces the need to make sure that we have all of the protective measures in place." The World Health Organization (WHO) recently said the disease may be spreading faster than originally thought and have declared a public health emergency of international concern

candidates.lead: A man who lost his wife to the disease says she would be proud of the charity mission in her memory
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Lauren Hirst North West Phil Jackson Phil Jackson, pictured with his wife Jen, has gathered a group of walkers for the challenge A widower who lost his wife to ovarian cancer said she would be proud of the charity mission that has been organi Jen Jackson, from Romiley, Stockport, died at the age of 62 in June, six years after being diagnosed with the disease Now her husband Phil - along with a group of friends and family - is set tackle a 200-mile (322 km) walking trail along the Greater Manchester Ringway over 15 days "She'd probably be shaking her head but deep down she'd be really proud of it," said Phil, a former goalkeeper for non-league clubs including Altrincham, Droylsden, Ashton and Cheadle Town The fundraiser - called Ova The Hills - is a nod to the age of the hikers who have an average age of 67 as well as name of the anatomy that the disease affects The walk will finish on the first anniversary of Jen's death on 25 June in Romiley where the couple lived

candidates.title: Rent caps needed to help homeless, Salford charity says
candidates.summary: Salford Loaves and Fishes says it has seen a rise in rough sleeping due to high rents
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review. | Validator: editorial_contract rejected item (story_type=planning, anchor=none, tier=filler).
candidates.practical_angle: Учитывать как изменение жилищной политики или поддержки жителей.
candidates.lead: Salford Loaves and Fishes says it has seen a rise in rough sleeping due to high rents
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Lois James BBC News, Salford BBC Jenni Smith said the Loaves and Fishes charity is supporting on average about 160 new people every month Rising rent costs are pushing more people into rough sleeping and the new Renters Rights' Act needed to The act came into force on 1 May and banned no-fault evictions, ended fixed-term tenancies and introduced new rules to stop bidding wars meaning renters cannot be asked to pay more than the advertised price Salford Loaves and Fishes, which supports homeless and vulnerable people in the city, said it was concerned it had not introduced caps on how much private landlords can increase rent The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) said it was "not looking at rent controls" as they could cause higher rents The latest government figures showed north-west England has seen a 20% increase in rough sleepers year-on-year , up from 367 in 2024 to 441 in 2025 The figures showed Salford had a particular spike, with an estimated count going from 18 in 2024 to 41 in 2025

candidates.lead: The cast of Finding Emily have also praised the people of Manchester for being "fantastic"
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Jasmine DuFraisse North West PA Media Finding Emily was released on May 22, but premiered in the Home cinema in Manchester weeks before. A director has said her decision to include so much of Manchester in her latest film was intentional and Finding Emily, which is set across Manchester was released on 22 May, and stars actors Angourie Rice and Spike Fearn as the leads It tells the story of how a club-night romance leads to a hunt through a university campus when a musician realises he has been given the wrong number by a love interest Director Alicia MacDonald, who describes Manchester as "the best city ever" said she wanted to "embed" Manchester into the fabric of the film and create what she calls a "love letter" to the city Finding Emily actor Spike Fearn has 'always been obsessed with Manchester' The centre of Manchester's Gay Village features heavily throughout, taking audiences inside venues like G-A-Y and Via, and offering a front-row seat to the city's nightlife Kat Ronney, who plays Emily Thewlis, spoke of her love of Canal Street and her first impression of the area, she said "I'd just arrived in Manchester and saw everyone so I just hung out. The people are just so fantastic."

candidates.lead: Image caption, Jenni Smith said the Loaves and Fishes charity is supporting on average about 160 new people every month Rising rent costs are pushing more people into rough sleeping and the new Renters Rights' Act needed to go further to support those in hardship, a charity has s
candidates.evidence_text: Image caption, Jenni Smith said the Loaves and Fishes charity is supporting on average about 160 new people every month Rising rent costs are pushing more people into rough sleeping and the new Renters Rights' Act needed to go further to support those in hardship, a charity has said The act came into force on 1 May and banned no-fault evictions, ended fixed-term tenancies and introduced new rules to stop bidding wars meaning renters cannot be asked to pay more than the advertised price Salford Loaves and Fishes, which supports homeless and vulnerable people in the city, said it was concerned it had not introduced caps on how much private landlords can increase rent The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) said it was "not looking at rent controls" as they could cause higher rents The latest government figures showed north-west England has seen a 20% increase in rough sleepers year-on-year , external , up from 367 in 2024 to 441 in 2025

candidates.summary: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." View 8 Images Pixie Rose Jones (Image: Manchester Family / MEN)

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Gay Village Attacked in the Gay Village, what happened next left him 'absolutely disgusted with police' Lewis Bell was brutally beaten as he was getting into a cab with his boyfriend, but says police waited too long to obtain CCTV of the incident View News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 06:14, 25 May 2026 Updated 06:25, 25 May 2026 A man who was brutally attacked at random while getting in a taxi in Manchester's Gay Village has hit out at the police after his case was closed, saying he feels 'absolutely disg Lewis Bell, 43, had been out with his fiancé Peter on April 4 and had visited bars in the village, including Via, The Goose and New York New York. The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

Chants of 'We are going up' echoed around the stadium amid the huge roar of cheers It comes as Bolton Wanderers have been promoted to the Championship after a 4-1 win over Stockport County. Wanderers took the lead early through Ruben Rodrigues. Adama Sidibeh had an equaliser ruled out for a foul by VAR but equalised on 30 minutes After the break, Bolton were the better side and took the lead when Kyle Wootton turned the ball into his own net. Sam Dalby made it three with a wonderful overhead kick to seal the result Stockport had Josh Dacres-Cogley sent off late for a hair pull on Ibrahim Cissoko. Rodrigues scored the penalty to seal the win Despite the sweltering weather , fans from both rival Greater Manchester sides made the big trip to Wembley for the play-off final in the hopes that they would see their team through to promotion

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Altrincham Erling Haaland has been her inspiration, but the NHS 'won’t give Phoebe that chance' Phoebe's only chance of long term survival is a liver transplant - but the NHS will not do it View 7 Images Erling Haaland has sent a message of encouragem Comments News Neal Keeling Chief reporter 17:29, 24 May 2026 Updated 17:33, 24 May 2026 He has been her inspiration. Now Erling Haaland has sent a heartfelt message to a little girl whose zest for life has not diminished despite needing a liver transplant which the NHS will not c Until last autumn Phoebe Clarke did martial arts, swimming, basketball, and rugby. And driven by watching Manchester City's number nine, she also played football for Broadheath Central under 9s, her local team in Altrincham Phoebe has a rare liver cancer called hepatic angiosarcoma which was diagnosed in February last year. Particularly aggressive, it can only be fully treated by surgically removing the affected areas; in Phoebe's case every part of her remaining liver is cancerous. She has had four Her only chance of long term survival is a liver transplant. But the NHS will not do it as the chances of her condition returning are considered too high View 7 Images Phoebe Clarke who is living with a rare form of liver cancer

Online videos of the young Leonardo Patrick might only be ten-years-old, but his talent for the guitar has brought him worldwide fame. The youngster, who is no stranger to performing, has been a familiar face on the streets of Manchester city centre since he was just five-years-old With nearly two million followers across social media, the Old Trafford-born rising star features in videos gigging to shoppers on busy Market Street . With taste older than his years, he's famed for performing hits by the likes of Elvis, The Verve, Oasis and Arctic Monkeys His popularity, with more than 220 million views on YouTube alone, has now stretched from their home of Greater Manchester to thousands of miles across the pond, with major TV execs even reaching out to offer appearances on the likes of America's Got Talent Put simply, all the youngster wants to do is 'have fun' performing to passers-by, and 'couldn't give two hoots' about his booming social media success, with profiles managed by parents Charbella and Matthew Patrick "He's been busking since he was five," Charbella, 41, told the Manchester Evening News . First Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter fashioned a deadly weapon by embedding a razor blade in the melted handle of a plastic knife Next the former New York gangster greased up his arms with baby oil to make restraint more difficult. Only then did he launch the brutal attack that left two Strangeways prison guards fighting for their life - and Jeter facing up to the prospect of spending the rest of his days b British-born Jeter moved to the US as a child but was deported back to the UK after getting involved with some of New York's most notorious street gangs In 2002, he was jailed for seven years in the UK for aggravated burglary after raiding a hotel room and holding a man at gunpoint. Just five years later he was arrested again after a 'honey trap' robbery went wrong, resulting in the murder of dad-of-seven Ghiass Tarabin in Hendon A trial at the Old Bailey heard the gangster's girlfriend Candy had unexpectedly turned up at the victim's home for a sexual liaison. But it was just a ruse. Jeter burst in demanding cash and valuables

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Northern Agenda Tap on at Bury and tap off at Bradford? How a Northern Oyster could still be on the cards The idea would allow passengers to travel round Greater Manchester and the North without buying multiple tickets Comments News Rob Parsons Execut Things were simple when planning my journey on a recent trip to London with my son, where I stayed near family in Lewisham in the south east of the capital but travelled to Wembley in the north west to watch some live sport Getting there involved multiple modes of transport including light rail and an underground system. But I was able to tap in at the start and off at the end with the same card knowing I'd only ever pay a maximum of £12.80 for my day's travel Fare capping as part of a single integrated transport system is part of what makes London's network work so well. And in the last 18 months, Greater Manchester's Bee Network has started to catch up, with an integrated "tap-in, tap-out" system now operating across its buses and Me Read more: The Metrolink line currently being dug up in £150m scheme But what about those who want to travel outside Greater Manchester, for example getting a tram from Oldham into Manchester Picadilly and then a train to Sheffield before catching a bus to South Yorkshire's cutting-edge Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre for work?

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." View 8 Images Pixie Rose Jones (Image: Manchester Family / MEN)

The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Football 'That third goal went in and I thought 'we’ve done it'- it brought me to tears' "It’s the fourth best day of my life behind meeting my partner and the births of my children" Comments News Amy Walker Court reporter and Charlotte Fisher Reporte Wembley Stadium was a cacophony of noise as Bolton Wanderers fans watched their beloved 'whites' into the Championship. Chants of 'We are going up' echoed around the stadium amid the huge roar of cheers It comes as Bolton Wanderers have been promoted to the Championship after a 4-1 win over Stockport County. Wanderers took the lead early through Ruben Rodrigues. Adama Sidibeh had an equaliser ruled out for a foul by VAR but equalised on 30 minutes After the break, Bolton were the better side and took the lead when Kyle Wootton turned the ball into his own net. Sam Dalby made it three with a wonderful overhead kick to seal the result Stockport had Josh Dacres-Cogley sent off late for a hair pull on Ibrahim Cissoko. Rodrigues scored the penalty to seal the win Despite the sweltering weather , fans from both rival Greater Manchester sides made the big trip to Wembley for the play-off final in the hopes that they would see their team through to promotion

Now Erling Haaland has sent a heartfelt message to a little girl whose zest for life has not diminished despite needing a liver transplant which the NHS will not c Until last autumn Phoebe Clarke did martial arts, swimming, basketball, and rugby. And driven by watching Manchester City's number nine, she also played football for Broadheath Central under 9s, her local team in Altrincham Phoebe has a rare liver cancer called hepatic angiosarcoma which was diagnosed in February last year. Particularly aggressive, it can only be fully treated by surgically removing the affected areas; in Phoebe's case every part of her remaining liver is cancerous. She has had four Her only chance of long term survival is a liver transplant. But the NHS will not do it as the chances of her condition returning are considered too high View 7 Images Phoebe Clarke who is living with a rare form of liver cancer

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News True crime series The New York gangster who vowed to 'christen Strangeways in blood' - then made friends with Charles Bronson Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter is one of the UK's most dangerous prisoners View 5 Images Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 15:52, 24 May 2026 Updated 16:07, 24 May 2026 The preparations were as precise as they were chilling. First Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter fashioned a deadly weapon by embedding a razor blade in the melted handle of a plastic knife Next the former New York gangster greased up his arms with baby oil to make restraint more difficult. Only then did he launch the brutal attack that left two Strangeways prison guards fighting for their life - and Jeter facing up to the prospect of spending the rest of his days b British-born Jeter moved to the US as a child but was deported back to the UK after getting involved with some of New York's most notorious street gangs In 2002, he was jailed for seven years in the UK for aggravated burglary after raiding a hotel room and holding a man at gunpoint. Just five years later he was arrested again after a 'honey trap' robbery went wrong, resulting in the murder of dad-of-seven Ghiass Tarabin in Hendon A trial at the Old Bailey heard the gangster's girlfriend Candy had unexpectedly turned up at the victim's home for a sexual liaison. But it was just a ruse. Jeter burst in demanding cash and valuables

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Northern Agenda Tap on at Bury and tap off at Bradford? How a Northern Oyster could still be on the cards The idea would allow passengers to travel round Greater Manchester and the North without buying multiple tickets Comments News Rob Parsons Execut Things were simple when planning my journey on a recent trip to London with my son, where I stayed near family in Lewisham in the south east of the capital but travelled to Wembley in the north west to watch some live sport Getting there involved multiple modes of transport including light rail and an underground system. But I was able to tap in at the start and off at the end with the same card knowing I'd only ever pay a maximum of £12.80 for my day's travel Fare capping as part of a single integrated transport system is part of what makes London's network work so well. And in the last 18 months, Greater Manchester's Bee Network has started to catch up, with an integrated "tap-in, tap-out" system now operating across its buses and Me Read more: The Metrolink line currently being dug up in £150m scheme But what about those who want to travel outside Greater Manchester, for example getting a tram from Oldham into Manchester Picadilly and then a train to Sheffield before catching a bus to South Yorkshire's cutting-edge Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre for work?

Mr Burnham has announced he's running for Parliament View 3 Images Andy Burnham (Image: M.E.N/Getty) News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:59, 24 May 2026 Updated 08:41, 24 May 2026 It was the kind of result a political party dreams of. Rewind to 2024, the last time Andy Burnham stood for re-election as Greater Manchester mayor, and the region was a resounding sea of red Of Greater Manchester's 215 wards only one - the Oldham suburb of Werneth - failed to cast their vote for the so-called 'King of the North'. His total of 420,000 votes was six times higher than that of the Conservative candidate who trailed in a distant second In an age when politicians are arguably more mistrusted and disliked than ever before, it's the kind of electoral stardust many in the Labour Party will be hoping to tap into as Burnham looks to defeat Reform in the Makerfield by-election - before possibly taking on Nigel Farage But just how popular is Andy Burnham in Greater Manchester and does that appeal translate outside his home turf? "Obviously in Greater Manchester he is very popular," said Andy Westwood, a professor of government practice at the University of Manchester . "You have to qualify it by saying it was a fairly low turn-out, but if you look at the mayoral election he won in every ward bar one

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Gay Village Attacked in the Gay Village, what happened next left him 'absolutely disgusted with police' Lewis Bell was brutally beaten as he was getting into a cab with his boyfriend, but says police waited too long to obtain CCTV of the incident View News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 06:14, 25 May 2026 Updated 06:25, 25 May 2026 A man who was brutally attacked at random while getting in a taxi in Manchester's Gay Village has hit out at the police after his case was closed, saying he feels 'absolutely disg Lewis Bell, 43, had been out with his fiancé Peter on April 4 and had visited bars in the village, including Via, The Goose and New York New York. The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

Chants of 'We are going up' echoed around the stadium amid the huge roar of cheers It comes as Bolton Wanderers have been promoted to the Championship after a 4-1 win over Stockport County. Wanderers took the lead early through Ruben Rodrigues. Adama Sidibeh had an equaliser ruled out for a foul by VAR but equalised on 30 minutes After the break, Bolton were the better side and took the lead when Kyle Wootton turned the ball into his own net. Sam Dalby made it three with a wonderful overhead kick to seal the result Stockport had Josh Dacres-Cogley sent off late for a hair pull on Ibrahim Cissoko. Rodrigues scored the penalty to seal the win Despite the sweltering weather , fans from both rival Greater Manchester sides made the big trip to Wembley for the play-off final in the hopes that they would see their team through to promotion

And driven by watching Manchester City's number nine, she also played football for Broadheath Central under 9s, her local team in Altrincham Phoebe has a rare liver cancer called hepatic angiosarcoma which was diagnosed in February last year. Particularly aggressive, it can only be fully treated by surgically removing the affected areas; in Phoebe's case every part of her remaining liver is cancerous. candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Stockport Unafraid to be ambitious, unafraid of change - why people from Stockport reckon it's different Different than its Greater Manchester neighbours; different in its culture; different in its politics View 7 Images Stockport (stock image) (Image News Edward Barnes Wirral Local Democracy Reporter 09:19, 23 May 2026 Updated 12:50, 23 May 2026 There's always been a sense among Stopfordians that the place is a little bit different. Different than its Greater Manchester neighbours; different in its culture; different, it seem Stockport’s ‘gold rush’ is only adding to its ‘unique’ identity and dynamic. There has long been something a little unusual about its politics, too Despite seeing a 2 per cent drop in their vote share, the Liberal Democrats were the only controlling party across Greater Manchester to see its councillor numbers increase as Labour lost a slew of seats elsewhere at the local elections earlier this month For the first time since 2011, the party now has a majority on Stockport council The local authority has been led by Liberal Democrats two times in the past, and the Conservatives between 1975 to 1983. Between those periods, no party has had a majority, with minority leadership passed between Labour and the Liberals since 1996

Officers were first called to concerns over a woman's welfare at The Rock car park in the town centre at 7.05am on Friday (May 22) Pictures from the scene showed a cordon in place and around five fire engines at the scene, along with police officers. It was the second incident at the shopping centre this week On Tuesday, emergency services attended another 'concern for welfare' incident, at 4.50am, and remained at the scene for almost 12 hours. The East Lancashire Railway says it has again been the victim of a 'deliberate' and 'disgraceful' act of vand Police are investigating after a locomotive and several coaches from the Inter City 125 heritage set were targeted in what the railway describe as a mindless attack, with extensive graffiti across the rolling stock In a statement the East Lancashire railway said "This was not opportunistic behaviour — it was a calculated act of destruction against irreplaceable heritage assets cared for by volunteers "Once discovered, volunteers immediately stepped in and worked tirelessly for several hours using specialist cleaning materials to remove the graffiti before it could permanently set "Their swift action prevented what could have been significant and lasting damage, although further polishing and restoration work is still required."

Credit: Jordan Pettitt/PA Mental health staff are set to strike over pay, UNISON has confirmed Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes after bosses ignored their own report and failed to improve staff pay, the union reports The drivers - who transport samples, medication, linen and food across Greater Manchester – say they have already completed a job evaluation that found their roles should be upgraded to a higher pay grade But the union says bosses have not acted on the findings and have instead asked for a second opinion from another NHS trust. It says 11 drivers have been left thousands of pounds out of pocket UNISON North West regional organiser Paddy Cleary said: "Trust bosses have failed to act in time and workers have been left with no choice but to strike. Nobody wants to take this step, but staff are underpaid for the vital work they do – as the trust’s own report makes clear "The job evaluation process was followed correctly. These staff should be on a higher rate of pay. Instead of putting this right, senior managers have chosen to delay the inevitable by seeking unnecessary advice

Credit: via Law of the Few The committee voted against officer recommendation to block Wain Estates’ Junction scheme, citing significant harm caused by the loss of an area of woodland among its reasons for refusal The 1m sq ft industrial and logistics development on 78 acres west of Isherwood Road – part of the former Shell petrochemical site in Carrington – features three sheds of 500,000 sq ft, 400,000 sq ft and 80,000 sq ft, as well as 17 acres of open storage space. Plans for the proje Trafford Council’s planning committee debated the scheme for an hour yesterday evening before deciding to reject the plans In recommending the project for approval, officers had identified a series of drawbacks but said the pros – job creation, a projected annual £100m GVA boost to the local economy, and a £12m contribution towards the creation of the £130m Carrington Relief Road – outweighed the con However, it became clear early on in the meeting that members of the planning committee felt the scheme would do more harm than good

candidates.lead: Iceni is leading the advisory team.
candidates.evidence_text: Iceni is leading the advisory team. Credit: planning documents A report has been lodged with the council seeking to clear the way for up to 390 homes off Starling Road in Ainsworth by the housebuilder The scheme is billed as Pilkington’s Fold, the name of the farmstead at the centre of the site. Taylor Wimpey’s project team is led by Iceni Projects, and also includes i-Transport and CSA Environmental The site is described by Iceni as being at the edge of the suburban area, close to the settlements of Radcliffe and Starling. It is a mostly rectangular site, narrowing to the north, with its southern boundary running up to the back gardens of houses fronting the Bolton-Bury Road Originally used for farming in the 18th century, the farmstead at the centre of the site is on Bury’s draft local list, potentially presenting a stumbling block In this non-designated heritage asset assessment, the development team is seeking to demonstrate that while a historic farmstead once stood at the heart of its proposed site, the buildings there now are more modern and don’t demonstrate any value meriting inclusion in the list

candidates.evidence_text: As the academic year draws to a close, schools across Greater Manchester are hitting that familiar, frenetic final stretch. It’s a period marked by exams, transition days, and the inevitable scramble to get everything signed and sorted before the summer break For those in their final year of primary or secondary school, it’s a milestone that carries a bit more weight than usual. And in the middle of all that activity, one tradition continues to stand the test of time: the school leavers hoodie It’s easy to dismiss them as just another piece of school apparel, but if you look at the culture in schools from Stockport to Salford, it’s clear they hold a distinct significance In a city where school identity is often tied to uniforms and strict dress codes, the leavers hoodie offers something different. It’s the one day (or, more accurately, the final few weeks) where students are given a bit of creative license For the student body, the hoodie acts as a badge of honor. It’s a visual acknowledgement of shared history, a collective nod to the years spent navigating everything from playground politics to exam stress. When a whole year group turns up in coordinated gear, it does more than j There’s also the practical, “Northern weather” reality of it. Manchester isn’t exactly famous for its dry, balmy evenings. A quality hoodie is, quite frankly, a necessity. But beyond the functionality, it’s about the memory attached to it

candidates.evidence_text: The Mill investigates a series of mudslinging anonymous email accounts ahead of next week's local elections Hey! Jack Dulhanty here , staff writer at The Mill. Our local election coverage aims to take readers deeper into the contests for council seats across Greater Manchester, focussing on key characters who are having big impacts on the city region’s politics. Today’s piece is on a f In late February, not long after the death of England’s longest serving councillor, David Lancaster, Salford’s Labour councillors received an email. It was to confirm that a by-election would be held for Lancaster’s seat — Barton and Winton — barely two weeks before the local ele This was unusual. When a seat is vacated so close to a local election, normal practice is to put it to the polls alongside the other seats set to be contested. But, if at least two members of an opposition party call for it, the council is compelled to put on a by-election. This Sign up for The Mill Manchester's quality newspaper, delivered via email “In all my years it has never happened,” says Barbara Bentham, a councillor in Salford who worked closely with Lancaster for years. “We usually wait until after the funeral.” Indeed, Lancaster’s family had hardly begun arranging his funeral when the by-election was called. candidates.evidence_text: They were donated to to Friends of Parks groups across Trafford Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Outgoing Mayor of Trafford Cllr Jane Brophy has marked the end of her mayoral year with a colourful gift to the borough's parks and green spaces Cllr Brophy donated around 100 hydrangea plants, purchased from Timperley's own Chris Walker Plants, to Friends of Parks groups across Trafford The plants were handed out over the bank holiday weekend across five hubs covering all corners of the borough, with the John Leigh Park Hub serving as the local distribution point for Altrincham and surrounding areas Parks and green spaces in the hub to receive plants included John Leigh Park, Stamford Park, Halecroft Park, Dunham Park, Navigation Road Park, Woodheys Park, Newton Park, Woodstock Park, Pickering Lodge, Timperley Green, De-Quincey Park, Denzell Gardens and Moss Park Gardens Local civic societies - including Altrincham in Bloom, Hale Civic Society, Timperley Civic Society, Altrincham Civic Society and Friends of Moss Park Gardens - also received plants

candidates.evidence_text: Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Households across Altrincham have just days left to renew their garden waste subscription - or risk gaps in their collections Trafford Council is urging residents to sign up before May 15th to guarantee they receive their new lilac 2026/27 permit in time for the new subscription year, which begins on June 1st The current yellow 2025/26 permits will no longer be valid after May 31st, meaning any bin without a lilac sticker on display will be skipped by collection crews Cllr Stephen Adshead, Trafford Council's Executive Member for Highways, Environmental and Traded Services, said: "Spring has well and truly arrived so there’s going to be more garden waste that needs collecting. Our collection teams will only collect garden waste from bins with a The council is also attaching reminder tags to bins across the borough The annual subscription price is unchanged from last year: £45 online or £50 by phone. The council has kept the online rate lower to reduce administration costs and encourage digital sign-ups

candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Paul Burnell North West Other The taxi driver did not immediately realise his car was on fire A van driver has told how he saved a taxi driver and his passengers who were oblivious to the fact their car was on fire The van driver, who does not wish to be named, said he was driving along the M60 in Greater Manchester near Simister Island on Thursday when he saw flames coming from the cab "I drove near him and told him he needed to pull over - he was not aware of the fire," the van driver said He added that one of the two passengers was disabled, so helped them out of the burning vehicle before dialling 999 to alert the emergency services The driver led all three people from the taxi into his van and drove to a safe area to await the arrival of the fire service "I was amazed how quickly the firefighters arrived," he said

candidates.lead: Following International Nurses Day , we held our first GMMH Nursing Awards on Monday 18 May 2026.
candidates.evidence_text: Following International Nurses Day , we held our first GMMH Nursing Awards on Monday 18 May 2026. The event provided an opportunity to recognise the outstanding contributions of nurses across our Trust and the impact they make on service users, families, colleagues and the wider After receiving over 300 nominations, we welcomed shortlisted colleagues to the celebration event at The Curve to celebrate our award winners, as well as recognise our highly commended runners up During the ceremony, we welcomed Tony Warne, Chair; Karen Howell, Chief Executive Officer; Carol Harris, Chief Operating Officer; and Salli Midgley, Chief Nursing Officer, to announce our worthy winners, who also shared their gratitude to all of our nursing colleagues, thanking t “It has been a pleasure to recognise colleagues for their contributions in GMMH over the past 12 months. These awards have been developed by nurses for nurses. It has been fantastic to see colleagues nominating each other, and I know the independent judging panel had a hard time “I also know that we have celebration events for Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) and other clinical colleagues. We are working on a similar event for our clinical support workers, who play such a vital role in delivering healthcare.” Without further ado, please join us in celebrating the winners and highly commended colleagues from the GMMH Nursing Awards 2026

candidates.evidence_text: We are proud to share that two members of our catering team impressed the judges at a national cooking competition, earning a silver medal and an award for the best dish of the day Amanda Barber and Sosana Sukhan, from our Prestwich site, competed in the Hospital Caterers Association (HCA) Chef Showcase, which took place at the Hotel, Restaurant and Catering Show (International Salon Culinaire) at ExCeL London on Monday 30 March 2026 The pair competed alongside six teams of NHS chefs from across the UK, all showcasing their culinary talents The challenge was to cook and present a three-course meal suitable for serving to patients in a hospital, meeting certain nutritional guidelines, costing £8 or less, within just one hour Dishes were judged by chef from across the hospitality industry Amanda and Sosana were awarded a silver medal and also picked up the newly created ‘Causeway Award’ for the best dish of the day. The award is named in memory of past winner Darren Taggart from Northern Ireland, who was a past Best-in-Class winner

candidates.lead: The British Medical Association (BMA) has announced that resident doctors (previously known as junior doctors) across England will take part in strike action from 7am on Tuesday 07 April until 7am on Monday 13 April Any GMMH patient or service user whose appointment is affected b
candidates.evidence_text: The British Medical Association (BMA) has announced that resident doctors (previously known as junior doctors) across England will take part in strike action from 7am on Tuesday 07 April until 7am on Monday 13 April Any GMMH patient or service user whose appointment is affected by the strike action will be contacted directly. If you are not contacted, we look forward to welcoming you for your appointment as planned During these strikes, other doctors (including consultants and other specialist doctors) will still be working. Staff will continue to provide patients with the best possible care under the circumstances, however, there is likely to be disruption to local services As with previous periods of strike action, we have tried and tested plans in place to mitigate risks to patient safety and manage any disruption We will continue to do everything we can to go ahead with planned appointments – especially for patients in greatest clinical need. Patients should attend their appointment as planned if they have not been contacted Regardless of any strike action taking place, it is really important that patients who need urgent medical care continue to come forward as normal, especially in emergency and serious life-threatening cases - when someone is seriously ill or injured, or their life is at risk

candidates.lead: Manchester will mark the ninth anniversary of the 22 May 2017 terror attack, which claimed 22 lives and left many others injured, this Friday
candidates.evidence_text: Manchester will mark the ninth anniversary of the 22 May 2017 terror attack, which claimed 22 lives and left many others injured, this Friday As usual the permanent Glade of Light Memorial, near Manchester Cathedral, will be the focal point for personal remembrance and reflection throughout the day. On the morning of Friday 22 May, the new Lord Mayor of Manchester Cllr Shaukat Ali, Council Leader Cllr Bev Craig OBE and The names of those who lost their lives will also be read out on 22 May during Manchester Cathedral services at 9.30am (Morning Prayers), 1.10pm (Holy Communion) and 4.30pm (Choral Evensong) The cathedral will be open throughout the day for those who wish to light a candle or pra Two one-minute silences will take place at Manchester Victoria Station during the day – at 12 noon and 22.31pm, the exact anniversary of the attack. The Railway Chaplain will offer at the station’s memorial after the midday silence “Manchester will never forget those who lost their lives in the 22 May 2017 terror attack, nor the many others who were injured or otherwise affected. Time passes but we will not allow their memories to fade “On the ninth anniversary of that terrible day, we also remember the remarkable solidarity shown by the city in refusing to give in to hatred - and the resilience and courage shown by the loved ones of those who were killed and by survivors.”

candidates.evidence_text: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen. We know this may raise questions or concerns for families In this article, we’re sharing clear and simple information about what these changes mean in Stockport, and where you can get advice and support. Some local details are still being finalised, and we’ll add updates as soon as they are confirmed. For more information, you can also We understand how hard it can be waiting for an assessment, and how difficult it can be to know where to turn for help in the meantime. That’s why we’ve introduced a new way of getting early support from the moment you first notice signs that your child or young person may be aut We know this is a lot to take in. If you’d like to find out more, ask questions, or talk things through, you’re very welcome to come along to one of our information drop-in sessions The Strategic Lead for Neurodivergence in Stockport will be attending the following drop-in sessions supported by the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS), Parents and Carers Together Stockport (PACTStockport), and Stockport Parents of ADHD Children in Education (SP In-person session with CAMHS and PACTStockport on Monday 11th May 10.30am-12pm, at Alma Lodge, 149 Buxton Road, Stockport, SK2 6EL. He was inspired by Liverpool’s success as the 2008 European Capital of Culture This year is Oldham’s turn, and its successful bid “Rising Up” celebrates the borough’s rich heritage, diverse communities and growing creative sector, with activity taking place across new and existing cultural venues and public spaces The town receives a £50,000 grant from the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) Culture Fund to develop a programme of cultural events and activities celebrating its unique heritage and bringing new opportunities for residents and visitors The programme will be built around three key themes

candidates.evidence_text: The historic lights on local landmark, Queen’s Park Bridge, are blazing once again, with final touches following its multi-million pound restoration now complete The bridge was recently partially rebuilt and restored by Heywood company, BDB Special Projects Limited, who used original drawings to carefully recreate the structure, so it has the same look and feel as it did when it was first unveiled in the 1930s As part of the project, specialists from Middleton company, Metcraft, were brought in to restore the beautiful gold and green lighting columns, which had once stood on the bridge. Although the lighting columns were beyond repair, Metcraft were able to salvage enough of the origin The lights were then reinstalled on the bridge and now have an electricity supply, enabling them to light up each evening from dusk to complement the existing street lights and highlight the bridge’s many heritage features The lights are energy efficient and can also be lit in different colours to honour special days in the calendar Councillor Shah Wazir, cabinet member for highways, said

candidates.title: Health column: Smoke Free Generation - A rare landmark moment for public health
candidates.summary: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.

candidates.lead: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
candidates.evidence_text: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tobacco to anyone born on, or after, 1st January 2009 - and, in doing so, creating the UK’s first ‘smoke‑free generation’ Make no mistake that this is one of the most significant public health reforms in a generation, and one that sends a clear signal: preventing harm before it begins matters Smoking remains the leading preventable cause of illness and early death in the UK. It significantly increases a person’s risk of cancer, heart disease and stroke, places huge pressure on the NHS, and continues to widen the gap in life expectancy across our communities The new law takes a bold, long‑term approach to breaking that cycle by ensuring today’s children never legally start smoking at all We know that information, advice and individual willpower can only go so far. Real, lasting change happens when policy and environments support people to make healthier choices

candidates.evidence_text: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign - set up by Leanne Lucas, a survivor of the Southport attack Nearly 20 council and partner buildings have already made the switch to the knives which feature a rounded-ended tip, as well as many residential homes taking up the pledge and making the swap Leanne Lucas said: “Replacing pointed tip knives is a simple but important step in reducing risk and preventing harm “Rounded tips make a real difference; the pointed tip creates the pressure point and increases the potential for serious injury “We also must recognise that harm is often about access and opportunity. When everyday items are easily available, they can be used in moments of crisis, impulse or vulnerability, so making small changes like this helps reduce that immediate risk

Each month, the group meets for a guided writing session, followed by a book club. You can join one or both sessions – and don't be put off coming if you didn't finish the book, you can still join in the discussion or The guided writing session runs from 6.30pm to 7.30pm. Suitable for all levels of experience, you'll be given prompts and can work on an existing project, or something new There's then a short break before an informal, welcoming discussion about that month's book, finishing at 8.30pm. candidates.evidence_text: Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence at the gallery where her practice has been influenced through the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programme, and visitors Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence in our Everyday Art School gallery where her practice is influenced by the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programmes, and visitors Sally is a socially engaged illustrator, textile artist and ceramicist with much of her work being inspired by the people around her Sally is particularly interested in social history and retelling the stories of ordinary people from the past. These have included Holocaust survivors, ‘witches’ and Victorian factory workers. She likes to link these stories to her own experiences, such as fostering refugees and Accessibility is at the heart of everything Sally creates. Such as writing audio descriptions in the form of poetry, writing easy reads, and delivering neurodivergent friendly tours You can find Sally most Thursdays in our Everyday Art School space on our Mezzanine

candidates.lead: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović .
candidates.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

Drawing inspiration from the Voyager deep-space probes launched in 1977 which dedicated a lifetime to venturing towards the unknown, Voyage attempts to pause the present moment and lead us into an uncharted Inspired by the repetitive actions of the traditional Japanese cup-and-ball toy ‘kendama’, Ryu Suzuki ’s Hakkō explores the gradual transformation of body and mind through focussed iterations of the simplest movements. The dancers enter a trance-like state of deep concentration, Korea’s only national contemporary dance company, KNCDC collaborates with artists of outstanding creative vision to create contemporary dance works that reflect history, society, and everyday life in modern Korea—resonating with audiences across regions and generations. In May 20 Young-doo Jung is a choreographer, director, dancer and actor who experiments with a wide range of forms and systems in choreography and composition, focusing on margins, breaths and subtle changes rather than excessive movement. candidates.lead: What's Love Got To Do With It?
candidates.evidence_text: What's Love Got To Do With It? Wed 27 May 2026 7:30PM Theatre Music Wed 27 May 2026 7:30PM Lyric Theatre Band A Standard £22.00 Access £22.00 Band B Standard £19.00 Access £19.00 Band C Standard £16.00 Access £16.00 Restricted View Restricted View £19.00 Restricted View 2nd Price After a sensational 2025 that saw over 60,000 Tina Turner fans across the UK singing, dancing, and having the time of their lives, What’s Love Got To Do With It? is back and bigger than ever for 2026! This electrifying tribute show celebrates the music and unparalleled career of Get ready for an unforgettable evening of high-energy, feel-good rock and soul, brought to life by a phenomenal live band. Packed with all the classic hits you know and love, the show delivers showstopping performances of Proud Mary, River Deep – Mountain High, Simply The Best, P Stepping into Tina’s iconic heels is Kent’s own Holly Bannis , the daughter of Greg Bannis , former lead singer of Hot Chocolate . Holly’s rise to center stage is as inspiring as it is exciting. Growing up in a musical family, Holly honed her craft for over a decade performing se “I had to audition as three of my musical icons, and Tina was an obvious choice for me. During lockdown, I studied her performances obsessively, getting into her moves, her walk, and her energy.” It wasn’t long before the team behind What’s Love Got To Do With It? noticed Holly’s raw talent and passion. Initially cast as a backing singer and understudy, Holly quickly stepped into the spotlight, making the lead role her own and earning standing ovations night after night

Meeting point for Tours is The Main Entrance. Please arrive 5 minutes before as late comers may not be admitted Routes may change and do, unavoidably, involve a number of stairs. candidates.evidence_text: For International Mother Language Day 2026 People’s History Museum launched Banner Bingo in an additional seven languages Alongside English, visitors can take their pick from Arabic, Chinese, French, Persian, Polish, Spanish, and Urdu as they take part in the free Family Friendly activity at the museum Banner Bingo is a quest to find a series of images featured within the colourful banners that are on display throughout the museum’s galleries. You can pick up a Banner Bingo card from the welcome desk, and after completing your search there is a small prize for taking part. Two Part of a programme of activities organised by Manchester City of Literature to celebrate International Mother Language Day The translations of Banner Bingo were provided by Peak Translations Ltd, thebigword, The Translation People and the Association of Translation Companies Are you more independent or community focused? candidates.evidence_text: PHM’s headline exhibition for 2026, On The Line: 100 years of strikes & solidarity (on show until 2 November 2026), is accompanied by an immersive programme of events exploring the exhibition’s themes and the stories represented in PHM’s nationally recognised collection Find out more about the exhibition and the full programme of events below Step into our new Family Friendly Strike Den where you can dress up, speak up, read stories, and create protest art, inspired by strikes and solidarity explored throughout the museum Explore strikes and solidarity and create textiles inspired by your discussions in this artist led workshop You are invited to power up your imagination and build your own mini robot from a simple sheet of paper! Inspired by the Deceptioncon robot costume currently on display in On The Line , which once stood on the picket line in support of workers’ rights Explore a century of struggles and stories of strength drawing on the museum’s nationally significant collections. This free exhibition is part of the national commemorations, marking 100 years since the 1926 General Strike

candidates.lead: The Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU) Human Sciences Seminar Series for 2026, Philosophies of Resistance, will explore the philosophical thought that has resisted colonial, racial and carceral oppression The 2026 series aims is to question the role that philosophical ideas
candidates.evidence_text: The Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU) Human Sciences Seminar Series for 2026, Philosophies of Resistance, will explore the philosophical thought that has resisted colonial, racial and carceral oppression The 2026 series aims is to question the role that philosophical ideas have played – and continue to play – in collective struggles for social justice, and human emancipation more broadly Frantz Fanon & Stand Up to Racism, Wed 25 March 2026 Each seminar includes an academic talk followed by an ‘in-conversation’ discussion between the speaker(s) and a local political activist, and questions from the audience The MMU Human Sciences Seminar Series is an invited speaker and research seminar organised by the Philosophy section of MMU’s Department of History, Politics and Philosophy The series, which has run for over forty years, was founded by Philosopher David Melling and Professor Wolfe Mays in 1979. It was created out of a desire to explore the various human sciences in a systematic way from the standpoint of critical philosophy

candidates.evidence_text: The Bulletin of the John Rylands Library is a prestigious peer-reviewed journal, published by Manchester University Press. For over a century it has published research that complements the Library’s world-renowned Special Collections Bulletin articles are broad ranging and multi-disciplinary, enhancing the scholarship and understanding of the collections. Areas of particular interest include, but are not limited to The editors invite the submission of articles in these fields and welcome discussion of in-progress projects. The editors also invite the submission of descriptive articles or shorter notices pertinent to items in the Library collections and those held in other institutions of th For further information, visit the Bulletin page on the Manchester Hive website Free access to full-text content from Volume 1 (1903) to Volume 80 (1998) is available through Library Search ("John Rylands Bulletin") Articles published since 1999 are available via Manchester Hive (subscription only). Further detail is available at Manchester Hive

candidates.evidence_text: A Lovely Weekend comedy festival Fairfield Social Club, Irk St, Manchester M4 4JT Fri 29th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 A Lovely Weekend is back this spring, taking over Fairfield Social Club for three days of stand-up, live podcasts and some of the sharpest names on the UK comedy circuit Running from Friday 29 May to Sunday 31 May 2026, the festival packs around 20 shows into one weekend, bringing together established favourites, fast-rising names and cult comics for what’s shaping up to be one of the most stacked comedy bills in the city Friday kicks things off with Harriet Kemsley, fresh from winning over a whole new audience on Last One Laughing, alongside viral stand-up Tom Lawrinson, whose offbeat delivery and internet-born chaos have made him one of the most recognisable new voices in British comedy Saturday sees the return of Mark Silcox, who sold out at the festival last year and remains one of the strangest and funniest acts around, plus the first ever live edition of Sean McLoughlin’s podcast The Sean McTalk Show Then on Sunday, Sean McLoughlin is joined by stand-up favourite Josh Pugh, newly announced SNL UK cast member Larry Dean and acclaimed French comedian and writer Celya AB, rounding off the weekend with a line-up that feels properly loaded from start to finish

candidates.evidence_text: No Art Open Air at Bolesworth Castle Bolesworth Castle, Tattenhall, Chester CH3 9HQ Sat 30th May, 2026 12pm - 11pm No Art is heading out of the city and into the grounds of Bolesworth Castle – not far from Chester – for what’s set to be the party brand’s biggest UK open-air show to date Taking place on Saturday 30 May, the one-day festival has been curated by Dutch duo ANOTR and marks the first time a No Art open-air event outside the Netherlands has expanded into a full two-stage format. That means a bigger site, more room for large-scale production and install ANOTR will headline the day themselves, joined by Marco Carola, Max Dean, Toman, Benja and Silvie Loto on the main stage, all artists who fit neatly into No Art’s polished, forward-thinking house world. Over on the second stage, things lean deeper and looser, with Chaos In The CB Set across 6,000 acres, the Cheshire castle site gives the event a lot more room to breathe than your standard field set-up. Organisers are promising upgraded sound, ambitious production and immersive art installations, with the countryside location also helping deliver a cleaner No Art has built its name by pairing house music with striking settings and a strong visual identity, with previous editions landing in Amsterdam, Barcelona, Miami, Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo. This one looks like its most ambitious move in the UK so far, and easily its biggest

candidates.lead: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel.
candidates.evidence_text: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel. So we went. And it more than lived up to the chatter Run by Demi Millard and Tom Pemberton, it’s a proper village pub with a very clear sense of place – rooted in the produce, landscape and flavours of the Lake District, but with cooking that goes far beyond what you might expect from a country inn. It felt like a natural fit for O On Saturday 30 and Sunday 31 May , The Royal Oak will head to Torr Vale Mill to collaborate with Tartuffe on an eight-course menu built around the Lakes , and the ingredients that define that part of the world The menu opens with Carlingford oysters, grilled over the Big Green Egg with wild garlic and crème fraîche, followed by a smoked chicken wing with Morecambe Bay brown shrimp – flavours that will immediately place you in the South Lakes At the centre of the evening is Hebridean lamb à la ficelle , served with white beans, anchoïade, courgette, dandelion, bitter leaf salad and The Royal Oak’s mint sauce. It’s exactly the kind of generous, elemental dish Open Fire Dining Club was made for

candidates.evidence_text: Yan Tan Tethera Farmer’s Market at Track Track Brewing Co - Brewery & Taproom, Unit 18, Piccadilly Trading Estate, Manchester M1 2NP Sun 31st May, 2026 12pm - 8pm Yan Tan Tethera Farmer’s Market is back for its May edition, and this time it’s turning the dial up a bit On Sunday 31 May, the market returns as a full Food Festival edition, bringing together farmers, growers, chefs, drinks, music and a very strong line-up of Northern kitchens for a one-day celebration of the start of summer Already confirmed are Cafe San Juan, Erst, Freds, Stockport Provisions, Track Beer and Slice Culture Pizza, with fresh oysters, street food and more names still to be announced In proper Yan Tan Tethera style, each food vendor will be paired with one of the market’s produce stalls to create special one-off dishes built around what’s in season. Think chefs and growers side by side, swapping ingredients, cooking over fire, and leaning into a bit of creati The market takes its name from the old Northern shepherd’s counting system, a nod to rural life, seasonal rhythms and the people who work the land. What started as a small, thoughtful farmers’ market has grown into a proper meeting point for farmers, cooks, artists and the local

candidates.evidence_text: After captivating the nation’s heart as the winner of Britain’s Got Talent 2017, and announcing the imminent release of his debut album ‘Our Generation’ on Syco Music November 17th, multi-instrumentalist, composer and producer, Tokio Myers recently announced a show at The Albert Tokio Myers is totally unique. He’s created a sound all his own, fusing an array of musical influences that have shaped him as an artist, ranging from classical and cinematic to hip-hop, dance and 90s’ soul Tokio grew up in North London before being awarded a coveted piano scholarship at the Royal College of Music. A stint as a studio musician led to recording and touring with some of the biggest names in music including Amy Winehouse, The Police, Kanye West and Mr Hudson. Now those Tokio has recently released the beautiful single ‘Angel’ from his hotly anticipated album ‘Our Generation’ and it is absolutely gorgeous!

candidates.evidence_text: Nathaniel Rateliff is a striking, emotive tenor vocalist and songwriter whose work runs the gamut from simple folk and Americana to roots rock & roll to vintage rhythm & blues. He and his band the Night Sweats are based in Denver, Colorado. After fronting two earlier bands, Ratel Rateliff had begun writing songs that required a band, so he formed the Night Sweats. Their rowdy retro sound was deeply influenced by vintage soul, R&B and gospel traditions, Van Morrison and the Band. A formidable live unit with a reputation for stage-quaking shows, they signed Nathaniel Rateliff & the Night Sweats’ self-titled debut album was issued in August 2015, preceded by appearances at the Newport Folk Festival and The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon. They toured globally in support of the album. While still on the road, they issued A Little S Be sure to catch Nathaniel Ratecliff and The Night Sweats live at Albert Hall on the 14th April 2018!

candidates.evidence_text: After captivating the nation’s heart as the winner of Britain’s Got Talent 2017, and announcing the imminent release of his debut album ‘Our Generation’ on Syco Music November 17th, multi-instrumentalist, composer and producer, Tokio Myers announces his first ever UK tour next sp Tokio Myers is totally unique. He’s created a sound all his own, fusing an array of musical influences that have shaped him as an artist, ranging from classical and cinematic to hip-hop, dance and 90s’ soul Tokio grew up in North London before being awarded a coveted piano scholarship at the Royal College of Music. A stint as a studio musician led to recording and touring with some of the biggest names in music including Amy Winehouse, The Police, Kanye West and Mr Hudson. Now those Tokio has recently released the beautiful single ‘Angel’ from his hotly anticipated album ‘Our Generation’ and it is absolutely gorgeous!

With thousands of performances across 50+ countries, they carry the living spirit of Sufi shrine music to global audiences, offering a rar Led by Tahir Qawwal and supported by a masterful ensemble of tabla, harmonium, and choral vocals, the group stays rooted in the classical Qawwali tradition-without fusion, electronics, or pop crossover. Each performance is a vibrant expression of spiritual passion, featuring the The atmosphere includes whirling Sufi dancing and a communal, ecstatic energy that transcends language or belief. Though born in the Sufi Islamic tradition, this music invites all people–regardless of background-into a shared space of unity and divine love.
